points: A civilisation is an advanced stage of human society characterised by organised government and administration, urbanism with planned cities, diverse crafts and trades, a writing system, cultural expression through art and architecture, and productive agriculture that supports both rural and urban populations.

Civilisations emerged at different times in different regions — Mesopotamia around 6000 years ago, Egypt a few centuries later, and the Indus-Sarasvatī civilisation around 4500 years ago (2600–1900 BCE), one of the world's oldest and most extensive early civilisations.

The Indus-Sarasvatī (Harappan) civilisation developed in the fertile plains of the Indus and Sarasvatī river basins, with major cities like Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, and Kalibangan showcasing advanced town-planning with wide streets, fortifications, distinct urban zones, standardised baked-brick construction, and sophisticated water management systems.

The Harappans possessed remarkable engineering capabilities, as evidenced by the Great Bath of Mohenjo-daro, elaborate drainage and sewage systems, hundreds of wells, and massive reservoirs at Dholavira measuring up to 73 metres in length, reflecting high civic sense and administrative organisation.

The Harappan economy was based on productive agriculture (barley, wheat, cotton, pulses), diverse crafts (carnelian beads, shell bangles, bronze tools), and extensive trade networks across land, rivers, and seas, with the dockyard at Lothal serving as evidence of intensive maritime commerce.

Archaeological discoveries of seals, figurines (including the famous 'Dancing Girl'), gaming boards, mirrors, and terracotta objects reveal a sophisticated material and cultural life; the civilisation declined around 1900 BCE due to climatic changes and drying of the Sarasvatī River, yet much of Harappan culture and knowledge were transmitted to later Indian civilisations.
